(українська мова, *ukrainska mova*, IPA: [[ʊkrɐˈjinʲsʲkɐ ˈmɔwɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian")) is an [East Slavic language](/wiki/East_Slavic_languages "East Slavic languages"), spoken primarily in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). It is the [first (native) language](/wiki/First_language "First language") of a large majority of [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians").

**Ukrainian** (українська мова, *ukrainska mova*, IPA: [[ʊkrɐˈjinʲsʲkɐ ˈmɔwɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian")) is an [East Slavic language](/wiki/East_Slavic_languages "East Slavic languages"), spoken primarily in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). It is the [first (native) language](/wiki/First_language "First language") of a large majority of [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians").

Written Ukrainian uses the [Ukrainian alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_alphabet "Ukrainian alphabet"), a variant of the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script"). The [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") is studied by the [National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences_of_Ukraine "National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine") and [Potebnia Institute of Linguistics](/wiki/Potebnia_Institute_of_Linguistics "Potebnia Institute of Linguistics"). Comparisons are often made between Ukrainian and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), another East Slavic language, yet there is more [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"),[[7]](#cite_note-classification145-9) and a closer [lexical distance](/wiki/Lexical_similarity "Lexical similarity") to [West Slavic](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") and [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language").[[8]](#cite_note-10)

Ukrainian is a descendant of [Old East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic"), a language spoken in the [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") state of [Kievan Rusʹ](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%CA%B9 "Kievan Rusʹ"). In the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), the language developed into [Ruthenian](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language"), where it became an official language,[[9]](#cite_note-11) before a process of [Polonization](/wiki/Polonisation "Polonisation") began in the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"). By the 18th century, Ruthenian diverged into regional variants, and the modern Ukrainian language developed in the territory of present-day Ukraine.[[10]](#cite_note-pugh-12)[[11]](#cite_note-shevelov-13)[[12]](#cite_note-bunčić-14) [Russification](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine "Russification of Ukraine") saw the Ukrainian language banned as a subject from schools and as a language of instruction in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and continued in various ways in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[13]](#cite_note-schools-15) Even so, the language continued to see use throughout the country, and remained particularly strong in [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine").[[14]](#cite_note-WUkraineL-16)[[15]](#cite_note-UkrGrammar30-17)

## Linguistic development

### Theories

Specific developments that led to a gradual change of the Old East Slavic vowel system into the system found in modern Ukrainian began around the 12th/13th century (that is, still at the time of the Kievan Rusʹ) with a lengthening and raising of the Old East Slavic mid vowels *e* and *o* when followed by a consonant and a [weak yer](/wiki/Havl%C3%ADk%27s_law "Havlík's law") vowel that would eventually disappear completely, for example Old East Slavic котъ /kɔtə/ > Ukrainian кіт /kit/ 'cat' (via transitional stages such as /koˑtə̆/, /kuˑt(ə̆)/, /kyˑt/ or similar) or Old East Slavic печь /pʲɛtʃʲə/ > Ukrainian піч /pitʃ/ 'oven' (via transitional stages such as /pʲeˑtʃʲə̆/, /pʲiˑtʃʲ/ or similar).[[16]](#cite_note-18) This raising and other [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") developments of the time, such as the merger of the Old East Slavic vowel [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") и /i/ and ы /ɨ/ into the specifically Ukrainian phoneme /ɪ ~ e/, spelled with и (in the 13th/14th centuries), and the [fricativisation](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative") of the Old East Slavic consonant г /g/, probably first to /ɣ/ (in the 13th century), with /ɦ/ as a reflex in Modern Ukrainian, did not happen in Russian. Only the fricativisation of Old East Slavic г /g/ occurred in Belarusian, where the present-day reflex is /ɣ/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Ahatanhel Krymsky](/wiki/Ahatanhel_Krymsky "Ahatanhel Krymsky") and [Aleksey Shakhmatov](/wiki/Aleksey_Shakhmatov "Aleksey Shakhmatov") assumed the existence of the common spoken language of Eastern Slavs only in prehistoric times.[[17]](#cite_note-19) According to them the diversification of the Old East Slavic language took place in the 8th or early 9th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Russian linguist [Andrey Zaliznyak](/wiki/Andrey_Zaliznyak "Andrey Zaliznyak") stated that the [Old Novgorod dialect](/wiki/Old_Novgorod_dialect "Old Novgorod dialect") differed significantly from that of other dialects of Kievan Rusʹ during the 11th–12th century, but started becoming more similar to them around the 13th–15th centuries. The modern Russian language hence developed from the fusion of this Novgorod dialect and the common dialect spoken by the other Kievan Rusʹ, whereas the modern Ukrainian and Belarusian languages developed from dialects which did not differ from each other in a significant way.[[18]](#cite_note-About_Russian_Language_History-20)

Ukrainian linguist [Stepan Smal-Stotsky](/wiki/Stepan_Smal-Stotsky "Stepan Smal-Stotsky") denies the existence of a common Old East Slavic language at any time in the past.[[19]](#cite_note-21) Similar points of view were shared by [Yevhen Tymchenko](/w/index.php?title=Yevhen_Tymchenko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yevhen Tymchenko (page does not exist)"), [Vsevolod Hantsov](/w/index.php?title=Vsevolod_Hantsov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vsevolod Hantsov (page does not exist)"), [Olena Kurylo](/wiki/Olena_Kurylo "Olena Kurylo"), [Ivan Ohienko](/wiki/Ivan_Ohienko "Ivan Ohienko") and others. According to this theory, the dialects of East Slavic tribes evolved gradually from the common Proto-Slavic language without any intermediate stages during the 6th through 9th centuries. The Ukrainian language was formed by convergence of tribal dialects, mostly due to an intensive migration of the population within the territory of today's Ukraine in later historical periods. This point of view was also supported by [George Shevelov](/wiki/George_Shevelov "George Shevelov")'s phonological studies,[[20]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-22) which argue that specific features[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] were already recognizable in the southern dialects of Old East Slavic (seen as ancestors to Ukrainian) as far back as these varieties can be documented.[[11]](#cite_note-shevelov-13)

### Origins and developments during medieval times

[YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") | |

As a result of close Slavic contacts with the remnants of the [Scythian](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians") and [Sarmatian](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians") population north of the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), lasting into the early [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the appearance of the voiced fricative γ/г (romanized "h"), in modern Ukrainian and some southern Russian dialects is explained by the assumption that it initially emerged in [Scythian](/wiki/Scythian_language "Scythian language") and related eastern Iranian dialects, from earlier common [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") *\*g* and *\*gʰ*.[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)

During the 13th century, when German settlers were invited to Ukraine by the princes of the [Kingdom of Ruthenia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ruthenia "Kingdom of Ruthenia"), German words began to appear in the language spoken in Ukraine. Their influence would continue under[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") not only through German colonists but also through the [Yiddish-speaking](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") Jews. Often such words involve trade or handicrafts. Examples of words of German or Yiddish origin spoken in Ukraine include *dakh* ("roof"), *rura* ("pipe"), *rynok* ("market"), *kushnir* ("furrier"), and *majster* ("master" or "craftsman").[[24]](#cite_note-Concise_Middle-26)

### Developments under Poland and Lithuania

In the 13th century, eastern parts of Rus (including Moscow) came under [Tatar rule](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Kievan_Rus%CA%B9 "Mongol invasion of Kievan Rusʹ") until their unification under the Tsardom of [Muscovy](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia"), whereas the south-western areas (including [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv")) were incorporated into the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"). For the following four centuries, the languages of the two regions evolved in relative isolation from each other. Direct written evidence of the existence of the Ukrainian language dates to the late 16th century.[[25]](#cite_note-27) By the 16th century, a peculiar official language formed: a mixture of the [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") standardised language of [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic"), [Ruthenian](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language") and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"). The influence of the latter gradually increased relative to the former two, as the nobility and rural large-landowning class, known as the [szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta"), was largely Polish-speaking. Documents soon took on many Polish characteristics superimposed on Ruthenian phonetics.[[26]](#cite_note-KostOstr-28)

[Polish–Lithuanian](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") rule and education also involved significant exposure to [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). Much of the influence of Poland on the development of the Ukrainian language has been attributed to this period, and is reflected in multiple words and constructions used in everyday Ukrainian speech that were taken from Polish or Latin. Examples of Polish words adopted from this period include *zavzhdy* (always; taken from old Polish word *zawżdy*) and *obitsiaty* (to promise; taken from Polish *obiecać*) and from Latin (via Polish) *raptom* (suddenly) and *meta* (aim or goal).[[24]](#cite_note-Concise_Middle-26)

Significant contact with [Tatars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars") and [Turks](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") resulted in many [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") words, particularly those involving military matters and steppe industry, being adopted into the Ukrainian language. Examples include *torba* (bag) and *tyutyun* (tobacco).[[24]](#cite_note-Concise_Middle-26)

Because of the substantial number of loanwords from Polish, German, Czech and Latin, early modern vernacular Ukrainian (*prosta mova*, "[simple speech](/wiki/Simple_speech "Simple speech")") had more lexical similarity with [West Slavic languages](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") than with Russian or Church Slavonic.[[27]](#cite_note-29) By the mid-17th century, the linguistic divergence between the Ukrainian and Russian languages had become so significant that there was a need for translators during negotiations for the [Treaty of Pereyaslav](/wiki/Treaty_of_Pereyaslav "Treaty of Pereyaslav"), between [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky"), head of the [Zaporozhian Host](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Host "Zaporozhian Host"), and the Russian state.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

By the 18th century, Ruthenian had diverged into regional variants, developing into the modern [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), [Rusyn](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language"), and Ukrainian languages.[[10]](#cite_note-pugh-12)[[11]](#cite_note-shevelov-13)[[12]](#cite_note-bunčić-14)

### Chronology

The accepted chronology of Ukrainian divides the language into Old Ukrainian, Middle Ukrainian, and Modern Ukrainian.[[29]](#cite_note-31) Shevelov explains that much of this is based on the character of contemporary written sources, ultimately reflecting socio-historical developments, and he further subdivides the Middle period into three phases:[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)

* Proto-Ukrainian (abbreviated PU, Ukrainian: *protoukrajinsʹkyj period*, until the mid-11th century), with no extant written sources by speakers in Ukraine. Corresponding to aspects of [Old East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic").
* Old Ukrainian (OU, *davnʹoukrajinsʹkyj period* or *davnʹoukrajinsʹka mova*, mid-11th to 14th c., conventional end date 1387), elements of phonology are deduced from written texts mainly in Church Slavic. Part of broader Old East Slavic.
* Middle Ukrainian (*serednʹoukrajinsʹkyj period* or *staroukrajinsʹka mova*, 15th to 18th c.), historically called [Ruthenian](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language").
  + Early Middle Ukrainian (EMU, *rannʹoserednʹoukrajinsʹkyj period*, 15th to mid-16th c., 1387–1575), analysis focuses on distinguishing Ukrainian and Belarusian texts.
  + Middle Ukrainian (MU, *serednʹoukrajinsʹkyj period*, mid-16th to early 18th c., 1575–1720), represented by several vernacular language varieties as well as a version of Church Slavonic.
  + Late Middle Ukrainian (LMU, *piznoserednʹoukrajinsʹkyj period*, rest of the 18th c., 1720–1818), found in many mixed Ukrainian–Russian and Russian–Ukrainian texts.
* Modern Ukrainian (MoU, from the very end of the 18th c., *sučasnyj period* or *sučasna ukrajinsʹka mova*, from 1818), the vernacular recognized first in literature, and subsequently all other written genres.

Ukraine marks the Day of Ukrainian Writing and Language on 9 November, the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") of [Nestor the Chronicler](/wiki/Nestor_the_Chronicler "Nestor the Chronicler").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Rusʹ and Kingdom of Ruthenia

See also: [Ruthenian language](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language")

The era of [Kievan Rusʹ](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%CA%B9 "Kievan Rusʹ") (c. 880–1240) is the subject of some linguistic controversy, as the language of much of the literature was purely or heavily [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Slavonic_language "Old Slavonic language"). Some theorists[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] see an early Ukrainian stage in language development here, calling it Old Ruthenian; others[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] term this era [Old East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic"). Russian theorists tend to amalgamate Rusʹ to the modern nation of Russia, and call this linguistic era Old Russian. However, according to Russian linguist Andrey Zaliznyak (2012), people from the [Novgorod Republic](/wiki/Novgorod_Republic "Novgorod Republic") did not call themselves *Rusʹ* until the 14th century; earlier Novgorodians reserved the term *Rusʹ* for the [Kiev](/wiki/Principality_of_Kiev "Principality of Kiev"), [Pereyaslavl](/wiki/Principality_of_Pereyaslavl "Principality of Pereyaslavl") and [Chernigov](/wiki/Principality_of_Chernigov "Principality of Chernigov") principalities.[[18]](#cite_note-About_Russian_Language_History-20) At the same time as evidenced by contemporary chronicles, the ruling princes and kings of [Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia") and Kiev called themselves "people of Rusʹ" (in foreign sources called "[Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians")"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and Galicia–Volhynia has alternately been called the Principality or Kingdom of Ruthenia.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJakubowskiMiszczukKawałkoKomornicki20166-35)

Also according to Andrey Zaliznyak, the Novgorodian dialect differed significantly from that of other dialects of Kievan Rus during the 11th–12th century, but
started becoming more similar to them around 13th–15th centuries. The modern Russian language hence developed from the fusion of this Novgorodian dialect and the common dialect spoken by the other Kievan Rus, whereas the modern Ukrainian and Belarusian languages developed from the dialects which did not differ from each other in a significant way.[[18]](#cite_note-About_Russian_Language_History-20)

### In the Grand Duchy of Lithuania (c. 1349–1569)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Peresopnytske_Gospel_04.jpg/250px-Peresopnytske_Gospel_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peresopnytske_Gospel_04.jpg)

Miniature of [St Luke](/wiki/St_Luke "St Luke") from the [Peresopnytsia Gospels](/wiki/Peresopnytsia_Gospels "Peresopnytsia Gospels") (1561)

During the 14th century, the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") gradually established control over most of present-day Ukraine, except for Galicia, which would end up within the [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") (since 1434 largely administered as the [Ruthenian Voivodeship](/wiki/Ruthenian_Voivodeship "Ruthenian Voivodeship")). Local autonomy of both rule and language was a marked feature of Lithuanian rule. In the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, administration evolved largely upon the legacy of [Kievan Rus' law](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27_law "Kievan Rus' law"). Old East Slavic became the language of the chancellery, known as "Chancery Slavonic", and gradually evolved into the [Ruthenian language](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language"). Ruthenian would be the prevailing language in the [Lithuanian Metrica](/wiki/Lithuanian_Metrica "Lithuanian Metrica") of the 15th and 16th century, and the original language of the [Statutes of Lithuania](/wiki/Statutes_of_Lithuania "Statutes of Lithuania") (1529, 1566, and 1588), that were only later translated into Latin, and then Polish.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In the Crown of the Kingdom of Poland (1569–1648)

Further information: [Ruthenian language § High Ruthenian (c. 1550–1650)](/wiki/Ruthenian_language#High_Ruthenian_(c._1550–1650) "Ruthenian language")

Polish rule, which came later, was accompanied by a more assimilationist policy. By the 1569 [Union of Lublin](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin") that formed the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, a significant part of Ukrainian territory was moved from Lithuanian rule to Polish administration, resulting in cultural [Polonization](/wiki/Polonisation "Polonisation") and visible attempts to [colonize](/wiki/Colonization "Colonization") Ukraine by the Polish nobility.[[34]](#cite_note-Mywebpages.comcast.net-36)

Many Ukrainian nobles learned the Polish language and converted to Catholicism during that period in order to maintain their lofty aristocratic position.[[34]](#cite_note-Mywebpages.comcast.net-36) Lower classes were less affected because literacy was common only in the upper class and clergy. The latter were also under significant Polish pressure after the [Union with the Catholic Church](/wiki/Union_of_Brest "Union of Brest"). Most of the educational system was gradually Polonized. In Ruthenia, the language of administrative documents gradually shifted towards Polish.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ukrainian culture and language flourished in the sixteenth and first half of the 17th century, when Ukraine was part of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, albeit in spite of being part of the PLC, not as a result. Among many schools established in that time, the Kyiv-Mohyla Collegium (the predecessor of the modern [Kyiv-Mohyla Academy](/wiki/Kyiv-Mohyla_Academy "Kyiv-Mohyla Academy")), founded by the [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") [Metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") [Peter Mogila](/wiki/Peter_Mogila "Peter Mogila"), was the most important. At that time languages were associated more with religions: Catholics spoke Polish, and members of the Orthodox church spoke Ruthenian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As witnessed by contemporary documents, such as the lexicon of [Pamvo Berynda](/wiki/Pamvo_Berynda "Pamvo Berynda"), there was a strong mutual influence between [Church Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic "Church Slavonic") literary varieties and the common speech of Ukraine during that time.[[35]](#cite_note-nim-37)

Polish has had heavy influences on Ukrainian, particularly in [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine"). The southwestern Ukrainian dialects are [transitional](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") to Polish.[[36]](#cite_note-Hull-38) As the Ukrainian language developed further, some borrowings from [Tatar](/wiki/Tatar_language "Tatar language") and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") occurred. Other languages which influenced Ukrainian speech during that time were [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language").[[35]](#cite_note-nim-37)

### In the Cossack Hetmanate and Tsarist Russia until 1800

Further information: [Ruthenian language § Late Ruthenian (c. 1650–1800)](/wiki/Ruthenian_language#Late_Ruthenian_(c._1650–1800) "Ruthenian language")

When the [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") arose in the mid-17th century, [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") remained a language of administration in the Hetmanate, and most Cossack officers and [Polish nobles](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta") (two groups which overlapped a lot) still communicated with each other using a combination of Latin, Polish and Ruthenian.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2003116-39) On the other hand, the language barrier between Cossack officers and [Muscovite officials](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia") had become so great that they needed translators to understand each other during negotiations, and hetman [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") 'had letters in [Muscovite dialect](/wiki/History_of_the_Russian_language#The_Moscow_period_(15th–17th_centuries) "History of the Russian language") translated into Latin, so that he could read them.'[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnyder2003116-39)

The 1654 [Pereiaslav Agreement](/wiki/Pereiaslav_Agreement "Pereiaslav Agreement") between [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") and [Alexis of Russia](/wiki/Alexis_of_Russia "Alexis of Russia") divided Ukraine between the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Tsardom of Russia. During the following century, both monarchies became increasingly intolerant of Ukrainian own cultural and political aspirations.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Ukrainians found themselves in a colonial situation.[[39]](#cite_note-41) The Russian centre adopted the name *[Little Russia](/wiki/Little_Russia "Little Russia")* for Ukraine and *Little Russian* for the language,[[40]](#cite_note-42) an expression that originated in Byzantine Greek and may originally have meant "old, original, fundamental Russia", and had been in use since the 14th century.[[41]](#cite_note-43) Ukrainian high culture went into a long period of steady decline. The Kyiv-Mohyla Academy was taken over by the Russian Empire. Most of the remaining Ukrainian schools also switched to Polish or Russian in the territories controlled by these respective countries, which was followed by a new wave of Polonization and [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification") of the native nobility. Gradually the official language of Ukrainian provinces under Poland was changed to Polish, while the upper classes in the Russian part of Ukraine used Russian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In Austrian Galicia and Lodomeria (1772–1918)

After the 1772 [First Partition of Poland](/wiki/First_Partition_of_Poland "First Partition of Poland"), when the lands annexed by the Austrian [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") were reorganised as the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria"), the Habsburg administration was initially surprised to find out that, apart from Poles, there were a lot of other people living in it whom they began calling *Ruthenen* ("Ruthenians" or "Rusyny").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788-44) They differed from the Poles in that the vast majority of them adhered to the [Greek Catholic faith](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") (organised as the [Ruthenian Uniate Church](/wiki/Ruthenian_Uniate_Church "Ruthenian Uniate Church")) rather than Roman Catholic, and that their liturgical language was [Church Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic "Church Slavonic") rather than Latin.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788-44) Most of them had not received much education; they used Ruthenian only as a spoken language, few could read or write, and those who did more often used Polish or (increasingly) German instead.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788-44) As Empress [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa") had introduced a general compulsory education (*Allgemeiner Schulzwang*) in 1774, and enacted it in newly-acquired Galicia and Lodomeria in 1777, the decision was made to produce Polish and Ruthenian textbooks that were used in elementary schools for those language communities.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788-44)

> "Language is the property of a people, and no one should forget the speech of their own folk."

– Ivan Mohylnytsky, *Information on the Ruthenian Language* (1829)[[43]](#cite_note-45): 56

Although some Ruthenian parish schools were established in some villages, and some printed primers and catechisms in Ruthenian were distributed there, the effects of Ruthenian-language education achieved very little until 1815.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788-44) That year, [Ivan Mohylnytsky](/w/index.php?title=Ivan_Mohylnytsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ivan Mohylnytsky (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9C%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%B8%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%86%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BD "uk:Могильницький Іван"); [pl](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iwan_Mohylnycki "pl:Iwan Mohylnycki")], a [Przemyśl eparchy](/wiki/Ukrainian_Catholic_Archeparchy_of_Przemy%C5%9Bl%E2%80%93Warsaw "Ukrainian Catholic Archeparchy of Przemyśl–Warsaw") [canon](/wiki/Canon_(title) "Canon (title)") who contributed to the establishment of a strong network of Ruthenian parish schools and a teacher training school, published a catechism at the Royal University of Buda entitled *Christian Learning in the Case of the Common Catechism for Parish Children*.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788–89-46) He followed this up in 1823 by a *Grammar of the Slovene–Ruthenian language* (never published), and in 1829 by his treatise *Information on the Ruthenian Language* (published both in Polish as *Rozprawa o ięzyku ruskim* and in Ruthenian as "Відомість о руском языці"), which represents the first scholarly study arguing that Ruthenian was a language in its own right, separate from Polish, Russian, and Church Slavonic.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201788–89-46) On the other hand, new educational regulations in 1818 determined that schools that were exclusively attended by children of Greek Catholic parents were to receive instruction in Ruthenian, whereas schools attended by children of both Roman Catholic and Greek Catholic parents had Polish as the [medium of instruction](/wiki/Medium_of_instruction "Medium of instruction").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789-47) Nevertheless, pupils at Ruthenian-instruction schools had to learn Polish as a second language as well.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789-47)

In higher education, Ruthenian was not valued as an equal language, and students were expected to learn and use Latin and Polish instead.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789-47) Students training to become Greek Catholic priests at the [University of Lviv](/wiki/University_of_Lviv "University of Lviv") did receive instruction in Ruthenian in the so-called "Studium ruthenum" according to Austrian regulations between 1787 and 1809, but it was not a fully-fledged course; instead, it was regarded as a temporary measure for students who did not yet know Latin.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789-47) Both its professors and alumni received only half the salary of their counterparts from the "Studium latinum", the number of students steadily decreased over the decades, and in 1809 the Ruthenians themselves requested the "Studium ruthenum" to be abandoned.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789-47)

In the first few decades of the Austrian period in Galicia, there was also confusion amongst both the Habsburg administration and educated Ruthenians about which variety of written Ruthenian to use: late Church Slavonic, literary Russian, traditional written Ruthenian, or something close to how Ruthenian was actually spoken in Galicia at that time.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201789–90-48) The Habsburg Imperial censor for Slavic publications, [Jernej Kopitar](/wiki/Jernej_Kopitar "Jernej Kopitar") (himself from Slovenia), encouraged Ruthenian authors to base their written language on the Ruthenian vernacular, and from December 1833 onwards, to write [Ruthenian in a Latin alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_Latin_alphabet "Ukrainian Latin alphabet") rather than Cyrillic.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201790-49) This initiated a discussion on Ruthenian identity, later called the ["First Alphabet War" or "Blizzard"](/wiki/Alphabet_War "Alphabet War").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201790-49) Although most Ruthenian intellectuals did respond by increasingly basing their writings on spoken Ruthenian, the majority of them defended the use Cyrillic over concerns of Polonisation.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201790-49) Nevertheless, they could not agree on various standardisation issues; three different Ruthenian grammars were published between 1834 and 1848, and none of them was widely adopted.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201790-49) Before 1848, no Ruthenian dictionaries were produced, no Ruthenian-language periodical press existed within Habsburg Galicia, and Ruthenian played no role as language of administration.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201790-49)

[![Prevalence of Polish or Ukrainian language in Galicia in 1910](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Galicja_language.png/330px-Galicja_language.png)](/wiki/File:Galicja_language.png)

Prevalence of Polish or Ukrainian language in Galicia in 1910

The [Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Austrian_Empire "Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire") changed everything: the native languages of most populations in the Empire, including Ruthenian, were accorded official status, and all laws in the Danubian monarchy would be published in these languages from 1 October 1849 onwards.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201791-50) Ruthenian would henceforth be used in local administration in the *Landesgesetzblätter*.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201791–92-51) From 1849 onwards, various official periodicals were established in Ruthenian, and the Interior Ministry stipulated in July 1849 that street signs in Lviv had to include Ruthenian versions.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201792-52) In October 1852, the Ministry of Justice also decreed that Ruthenian could be used by parties involved in legal issues in their communication with the courts of law, although it would take until 1861 to allow these letters to employ *[skoropys](/wiki/Ukrainian_skoropys "Ukrainian skoropys")* Cyrillic rather than Latin script (or the Muscovite *graždanka* variety of Cyrillic).[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201792-52) In the post-1848 era, there were some contradictory developments, some of which countered Polonisation in the sphere of education, while others stimulated further Polonisation in the sphere of administration.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201794–95-53) Similarly, [Galician Russophilia](/wiki/Galician_Russophilia "Galician Russophilia") or Moscophilia strived towards ever greater assimilation of Ruthenian towards the so-called "Great Russian" language as used in Moscow, which still heavily leaned on Church Slavonic. Both the Habsburg administration and Greek Catholic Church raised concerns that these were "barely comprehensible" to the common people of Galicia and hampered the "development of the Ruthenian language", adding that Orthodox Imperial Russia was a threat to the overwhelmingly Catholic Habsburg realm.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201792–94-54)

Matters once again came to a head in May 1859, when the Polish governor of Galicia [Gołuchowski](/wiki/Agenor_Romuald_Go%C5%82uchowski "Agenor Romuald Gołuchowski") recommended Czech linguist [Josef Jireček](/wiki/Josef_Jire%C4%8Dek "Josef Jireček")'s proposal for a Ruthenian Latin alphabet, leading to the ["Second Alphabet War" or "Blizzard"](/wiki/Alphabet_War "Alphabet War").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201794-55) Ruthenian intellectuals almost unanimously rejected the proposal for fear of Polonisation, leading the government to overreact by banning the "Russian script" (meaning the Muscovite *graždanka*) in July 1859, which Ruthenian writers generally ignored.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201794-55) By March 1861, the Habsburg State Ministry essentially conceded defeat by stating that the Ruthenians themselves were responsible for developing their own language, and that it was not up to the government.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201794–95-53) Around the same time, however, Ruthenian intellectuals became acquainted with the writings of Ukrainian intellectuals from "Little Russia" in the Russian Empire, such as poet [Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko") (died 1861), who was fiercely anti-Russian and [Ukrainophile](/wiki/Ukrainophilia "Ukrainophilia"), leading many Galician Ruthenians to abandon their earlier Russophilia.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201795-56) In between the pro-Polish and pro-Russian tendencies, the Ruthenian language in Galicia would gradually develop into an independent literary and intellectual written language in the second half of the 19th century, when it was increasingly called "Ukrainian".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser201795–97-57)

### In the Russian Empire

During the 19th century, a [revival of Ukrainian self-identification](/wiki/Ukrainian_National_Revival "Ukrainian National Revival") manifested in the literary classes of both Russian-Empire [Dnieper Ukraine](/wiki/Dnieper_Ukraine "Dnieper Ukraine") and Austrian [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)"). The [Brotherhood of Sts Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/Brotherhood_of_Sts_Cyril_and_Methodius "Brotherhood of Sts Cyril and Methodius") in Kyiv applied an old word for the Cossack motherland, *Ukrajina*, as a self-appellation for the nation of Ukrainians, and *Ukrajinsʹka mova* for the language. Many writers published works in the Romantic tradition of Europe demonstrating that Ukrainian was not merely a language of the village but suitable for literary pursuits.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

However, in the Russian Empire expressions of Ukrainian culture and especially language were repeatedly persecuted for fear that a self-aware Ukrainian nation would threaten the unity of the empire. In 1804 Ukrainian as a subject and language of instruction was banned from schools.[[13]](#cite_note-schools-15) In 1817, the Synod of the Russian Empire issued a decree to close the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)

In 1847 the Brotherhood of St Cyril and Methodius was terminated. The same year [Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko") was arrested, exiled for ten years, and banned for political reasons from writing and painting. In 1862 [Pavlo Chubynsky](/wiki/Pavlo_Chubynsky "Pavlo Chubynsky") was exiled for seven years to [Arkhangelsk](/wiki/Arkhangelsk "Arkhangelsk"). The Ukrainian magazine *[Osnova](/wiki/Osnova "Osnova")* was discontinued. In 1863, the tsarist interior minister [Pyotr Valuyev](/wiki/Pyotr_Valuyev "Pyotr Valuyev") proclaimed in [his decree](/wiki/Valuyevsky_Ukaz "Valuyevsky Ukaz") that "there never has been, is not, and never can be a separate Little Russian language".[[59]](#cite_note-Valuyev-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)

Although the [name of Ukraine](/wiki/Name_of_Ukraine "Name of Ukraine") is known since 1187, it was not applied to the language until the mid-19th century.[[61]](#cite_note-Flier,Graziosi-63) The [linguonym](/wiki/Linguonym "Linguonym") *Ukrainian language* appears in [Yakub Holovatsky](/wiki/Yakub_Holovatsky "Yakub Holovatsky")'s book from 1849,[[62]](#cite_note-64) listed there as a variant name of the *Little Russian language*. In a private letter from 1854, Taras Shevchenko lauds "our splendid Ukrainian language".[[63]](#cite_note-65) Valuyev's decree from 1863 derides the "Little Russian" language throughout, but also mentions "the so-called Ukrainian language" once.[[59]](#cite_note-Valuyev-61) In Galicia, the earliest applications of the term *Ukrainian* to the language were in the hyphenated names *Ukrainian-Ruthenian* (1866, by [Paulin Święcicki](/wiki/Paulin_%C5%9Awi%C4%99cicki "Paulin Święcicki")) or *Ruthenian-Ukrainian* (1871, by [Panteleimon Kulish](/wiki/Panteleimon_Kulish "Panteleimon Kulish") and [Ivan Puluj](/wiki/Ivan_Puluj "Ivan Puluj")), with non-hyphenated *Ukrainian language* appearing shortly thereafter (in 1878, by [Mykhailo Drahomanov](/wiki/Mykhailo_Drahomanov "Mykhailo Drahomanov")).[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser2017103-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67)

A following ban on Ukrainian books led to [Alexander II](/wiki/Alexander_II_of_Russia "Alexander II of Russia")'s secret [Ems Ukaz](/wiki/Ems_Ukaz "Ems Ukaz"), which prohibited publication and importation of most Ukrainian-language books, public performances and lectures, and even banned the printing of Ukrainian texts accompanying musical scores.[[66]](#cite_note-68) A period of leniency after 1905 was followed by another strict ban in 1914, which also affected Russian-occupied Galicia.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuckyj199024–25-69)

For much of the 19th century the Austrian authorities demonstrated some preference for Polish culture, but the Ukrainians were relatively free to partake in their own cultural pursuits in [Halychyna](/wiki/Halychyna "Halychyna") and [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), where Ukrainian was widely used in education and official documents.[[68]](#cite_note-70) The suppression by Russia hampered the literary development of the Ukrainian language in [Dnipro Ukraine](/wiki/Dnieper_Ukraine "Dnieper Ukraine"), but there was a constant exchange with Halychyna, and many works were published under Austria and smuggled to the east.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the time of the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") of 1917 and the [collapse of Austro-Hungary](/wiki/Austro-Hungary#Dissolution_of_the_Empire "Austro-Hungary") in 1918, Ukrainians were ready to openly develop a body of national literature, institute a Ukrainian-language educational system, and form an independent state (the [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic"), shortly joined by the [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic")). During this brief independent statehood the stature and use of Ukrainian greatly improved.[[15]](#cite_note-UkrGrammar30-17)

Although in the rural regions of the Ukrainian provinces, 80% of the inhabitants said that Ukrainian was their native language in the Census of 1897 (for which the results are given above), in the urban regions only 32.5% of the population claimed Ukrainian as their native language. For example, in [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") (then part of the Russian Empire), at the time the largest city in the territory of current Ukraine, only 5.6% of the population said Ukrainian was their native language.[[71]](#cite_note-George-73)

Until the 1920s the urban population in Ukraine grew faster than the number of Ukrainian speakers. This implies that there was a (relative) decline in the use of Ukrainian language. For example, in Kyiv, the number of people stating that Ukrainian was their native language declined from 30.3% in 1874 to 16.6% in 1917.[[71]](#cite_note-George-73)

### Soviet era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Soviet_UA_class_union.gif/250px-Soviet_UA_class_union.gif)](/wiki/File:Soviet_UA_class_union.gif)

A Ukrainian-language poster reading "The social foundation of the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR") is an unbreakable union of the workers, peasants and [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia")"

During the seven-decade-long [Soviet era](/wiki/Soviet_era "Soviet era"), the Ukrainian language held the formal position of the principal local language in the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR").[[72]](#cite_note-life-74) However, practice was often a different story:[[72]](#cite_note-life-74) Ukrainian always had to compete with Russian, and the attitudes of the Soviet leadership towards Ukrainian varied from encouragement and tolerance to de facto banishment.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Officially, there was no [state language](/wiki/State_language "State language") in the Soviet Union until the very end when it was proclaimed in 1990 that Russian language was the all-Union state language and that the constituent [republics](/wiki/Soviet_republics "Soviet republics") had rights to declare additional state languages within their jurisdictions.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Still it was implicitly understood in the hopes of minority nations that Ukrainian would be used in the Ukrainian SSR, [Uzbek](/wiki/Uzbek_language "Uzbek language") would be used in the [Uzbek SSR](/wiki/Uzbek_SSR "Uzbek SSR"), and so on.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, Russian was used as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in all parts of the Soviet Union and a special term, "a language of inter-ethnic communication", was coined to denote its status.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Stalin

See also: [Ukrainianization](/wiki/Ukrainianization "Ukrainianization")

#### Khrushchev thaw

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Rouble-1961-Paper-1-Reverse.jpg/330px-Rouble-1961-Paper-1-Reverse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rouble-1961-Paper-1-Reverse.jpg)

While Russian was a de facto official language of the Soviet Union in all but formal name, all national languages were proclaimed equal. The name and denomination of [Soviet banknotes](/wiki/Soviet_rouble "Soviet rouble") were listed in the languages of all fifteen [Soviet republics](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union"). On this 1961 1 Rbl note, the Ukrainian for "one rouble", один карбованець (*odyn karbovanetsʹ*), directly follows the Russian один рубль (*odin rublʹ*).

After the death of Stalin (1953), a general policy of relaxing the language policies of the past was implemented (1958 to 1963). The [Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") era which followed saw a policy of relatively lenient concessions to development of the languages at the local and republic level, though its results in Ukraine did not go nearly as far as those of the Soviet policy of Ukrainianization in the 1920s. Journals and encyclopedic publications advanced in the Ukrainian language during the Khrushchev era, as well as [transfer of Crimea](/wiki/Transfer_of_Crimea "Transfer of Crimea") under Ukrainian SSR jurisdiction.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Yet, the 1958 school reform that allowed parents to choose the language of primary instruction for their children, unpopular among the circles of the national intelligentsia in parts of the USSR, meant that non-Russian languages would slowly give way to Russian in light of the pressures of survival and advancement. The gains of the past, already largely reversed by the Stalin era, were offset by the liberal attitude towards the requirement to study the local languages (the requirement to study Russian remained).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Parents were usually free to choose the language of study of their children (except in few areas where attending the Ukrainian school might have required a long daily commute) and they often chose Russian, which reinforced the resulting Russification. In this sense, some analysts argue that it was not the "oppression" or "persecution", but rather the *lack of [protection](/wiki/Linguistic_protectionism "Linguistic protectionism")* against the expansion of Russian language that contributed to the relative decline of Ukrainian in the 1970s and 1980s. According to this view, it was inevitable that successful careers required a good command of Russian, while knowledge of Ukrainian was not vital, so it was common for Ukrainian parents to send their children to Russian-language schools, even though Ukrainian-language schools were usually available.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The number of students in Russian-language in Ukraine schools was constantly increasing, from 14 percent in 1939 to more than 30 percent in 1962.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

#### Shelest period

The Communist Party leader from 1963 to 1972, [Petro Shelest](/wiki/Petro_Shelest "Petro Shelest"), pursued a policy of defending Ukraine's interests within the Soviet Union against [chauvinism](/wiki/Great_Russian_chauvinism "Great Russian chauvinism"). While he rejected Ukrainian nationalism, Shelest strongly promoted the use of the Ukrainian language and developed plans to expand the role of Ukrainian in higher education. He was removed, however, after only a brief tenure, for being too lenient on Ukrainian nationalism.[[75]](#cite_note-kuromiya-77): 42–43

#### Shcherbytsky period

The new party boss from 1972 to 1989, [Volodymyr Shcherbytsky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Shcherbytsky "Volodymyr Shcherbytsky"), purged the local party, was fierce in suppressing dissent, and significantly reduced the usage of Ukrainian in public functions in comparison to Shelest.[[76]](#cite_note-78) While Shcherbytsky still "defended the use of the Ukrainian language, promoted Ukraine’s particular interests, and defended certain aspects of Ukrainian culture, such as [Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko")’s poetry",[[75]](#cite_note-kuromiya-77): 43 he also shifted towards speaking Russian instead of Ukrainian in official settings.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

#### Gorbachev and perebudova

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Map12_b.png/250px-Map12_b.png)](/wiki/File:Map12_b.png)

Fluency in Ukrainian (purple column) and Russian (blue column) in 1989 and 2001

The management of dissent by the local [Ukrainian Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_(Bolshevik)_of_Ukraine "Communist Party (Bolshevik) of Ukraine") was more fierce and thorough than in other parts of the Soviet Union. As a result, at the start of the [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") reforms [perebudova](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika") and [hlasnist’](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost") (Ukrainian for *perestroika* and *glasnost*), Ukraine under Shcherbytsky was slower to liberalize than Russia itself.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Although Ukrainian still remained the native language for the majority in the nation on the eve of Ukrainian independence, a significant share of ethnic Ukrainians were russified. In [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk") there were no Ukrainian language schools and in Kyiv only a quarter of children went to Ukrainian language schools.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

The Russian language was the dominant vehicle, not just of government function, but of the media, commerce, and modernity itself. This was substantially less the case for western Ukraine, which escaped the [artificial famine](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor"), [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge"), and most of [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"). And this region became the center of a hearty, if only partial, renaissance of the Ukrainian language during independence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Independence in the modern era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Ukraine_census_2001_Ukrainian.svg/500px-Ukraine_census_2001_Ukrainian.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ukraine_census_2001_Ukrainian.svg)

Percentage of people with Ukrainian as their native language according to 2001 census (by region)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Inf_board_metro.JPG/500px-Inf_board_metro.JPG)](/wiki/File:Inf_board_metro.JPG)

Modern signs in the [Kyiv Metro](/wiki/Kyiv_Metro "Kyiv Metro") are in Ukrainian. The evolution in their language followed the changes in the language policies in post-war Ukraine. Originally, all signs and voice announcements in the metro were in Ukrainian, but their language was changed to Russian in the early 1980s, at the height of Shcherbytsky's gradual Russification. In the [perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika") liberalization of the late 1980s, the signs were changed to bilingual. This was accompanied by bilingual voice announcements in the trains. In the early 1990s, both signs and voice announcements were changed again from bilingual to Ukrainian-only during the [de-russification](/wiki/De-Russification "De-Russification") campaign that followed Ukraine's independence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since 2012 the signs have been in both Ukrainian and English.

Since 1991, Ukrainian has been the official state language in Ukraine, and the state administration implemented government policies to broaden the use of Ukrainian. The educational system in Ukraine has been transformed over the first decade of independence from a system that is partly Ukrainian to one that is overwhelmingly so. The government has also mandated a progressively increased role for Ukrainian in the media and commerce.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [2001 census](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)"), 67.5% of the country's population named Ukrainian as their native language (a 2.8% increase from 1989), while 29.6% named Russian (a 3.2% decrease).[[79]](#cite_note-81) For many Ukrainians (of various ethnic origins), the term *[native language](/wiki/Native_language "Native language")* may not necessarily associate with the language they use more frequently. The overwhelming majority of ethnic Ukrainians consider the Ukrainian language *native*, including those who often speak Russian.[[80]](#cite_note-2001ukrcensusKievLang-82)

According to the official 2001 census data, 92.3% of Kyiv region population responded "Ukrainian" to the *native language* (*ridna mova*) census question, compared with 88.4% in 1989, and 7.2% responded "Russian".[[80]](#cite_note-2001ukrcensusKievLang-82)

In 2019, the [law of Ukraine "On protecting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the state language"](/wiki/Law_of_Ukraine_%22On_protecting_the_functioning_of_the_Ukrainian_language_as_the_state_language%22 "Law of Ukraine \"On protecting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the state language\"") was approved by the parliament, formalizing rules governing the usage of the language and introducing penalties for violations.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

## Literature and literary language

Further information: [Ukrainian literature](/wiki/Ukrainian_literature "Ukrainian literature")

The literary Ukrainian language, which was preceded by Old East Slavic literature, may be subdivided into two stages: during the 12th to 18th centuries what in Ukraine is referred to as "Old Ukrainian", but elsewhere, and in contemporary sources, is known as the Ruthenian language, and from the end of the 18th century to the present what in Ukraine is known as "Modern Ukrainian", but elsewhere is known as just Ukrainian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Influential literary figures in the development of modern Ukrainian literature include the philosopher [Hryhorii Skovoroda](/wiki/Hryhorii_Skovoroda "Hryhorii Skovoroda"), [Ivan Kotlyarevsky](/wiki/Ivan_Kotlyarevsky "Ivan Kotlyarevsky"), [Mykola Kostomarov](/wiki/Mykola_Kostomarov "Mykola Kostomarov"), [Mykhailo Kotsiubynsky](/wiki/Mykhailo_Kotsiubynsky "Mykhailo Kotsiubynsky"), Shevchenko, [Ivan Franko](/wiki/Ivan_Franko "Ivan Franko"), and [Lesia Ukrainka](/wiki/Lesia_Ukrainka "Lesia Ukrainka"). The earliest literary work in the Ukrainian language was recorded in 1798 when Kotlyarevsky, a playwright from [Poltava](/wiki/Poltava "Poltava") in southeastern Ukraine, published his [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poem "Epic poem"), *Eneyida*, a [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque_(literature) "Burlesque (literature)") in Ukrainian, based on [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'s *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")*. His book was published in vernacular Ukrainian in a satirical way to avoid being censored, and is the earliest known Ukrainian published book to survive through Imperial and, later, Soviet policies on the Ukrainian language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Kotlyarevsky's work and that of another early writer using the Ukrainian vernacular language, [Petro Artemovsky](/w/index.php?title=Petro_Artemovsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Petro Artemovsky (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%93%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BA-%D0%90%D1%80%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE_%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87 "uk:Гулак-Артемовський Петро Петрович")], used [the southeastern dialect spoken in the Poltava, Kharkiv and southern Kyiv regions of the Russian Empire](/wiki/Middle_Dnieprian_dialect "Middle Dnieprian dialect"). This dialect would serve as the basis of the Ukrainian literary language when it was developed by Shevchenko and Kulish in the mid 19th century. In order to raise its status from that of a dialect to that of a language, various elements from folklore and traditional styles were added to it.[[82]](#cite_note-Shevelov1981-84)

The Ukrainian literary language developed further when the Russian state banned the use of the Ukrainian language, prompting many of its writers to move to the western Ukrainian region of Galicia which was under more liberal Austrian rule; after the 1860s the majority of Ukrainian literary works were published in Austrian Galicia. During this period Galician influences were adopted in the Ukrainian literary language, particularly with respect to vocabulary involving law, government, technology, science, and administration.[[82]](#cite_note-Shevelov1981-84)

## Current usage

Further information: [Law of Ukraine "On supporting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the State language"](/wiki/Law_of_Ukraine_%22On_supporting_the_functioning_of_the_Ukrainian_language_as_the_State_language%22 "Law of Ukraine \"On supporting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the State language\"")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Ivan_Franko_Museum_in_Krivorivnia.jpg/250px-Ivan_Franko_Museum_in_Krivorivnia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivan_Franko_Museum_in_Krivorivnia.jpg)

Ukrainian language traffic sign for the Ivan Franko Museum in [Kryvorivnia](/wiki/Kryvorivnia "Kryvorivnia")

The use of the Ukrainian language is increasing after a long period of decline. Although there are almost fifty million ethnic [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") worldwide, including 37.5 million in Ukraine in 2001 (77.8% of the total population at the time), the Ukrainian language is prevalent mainly in western and central Ukraine. In Kyiv, both Ukrainian and Russian are spoken, a notable shift from the recent past when the city was primarily Russian-speaking.[[83]](#cite_note-d-m.com.ua-85)

The shift is believed to be caused mainly by an influx of migrants from western regions of Ukraine but also by some Kyivans opting to use the language they speak at home more widely in public settings. Public signs and announcements in Kyiv are displayed in Ukrainian. In southern and eastern Ukraine, Russian is the prevalent language in most large and some small cities. According to the Ukrainian Census of 2001, 87.8% of people living in Ukraine were fluent in Ukrainian.[[83]](#cite_note-d-m.com.ua-85)

In August 2022, a survey in Ukraine by Rating Group found that 85% said they speak Ukrainian or Ukrainian and Russian at home, 51% only Ukrainian, an increase from 61% and 44% in February 2014.[[84]](#cite_note-:0-86)[[85]](#cite_note-:1-87) In the same survey, 76% considered Ukrainian their native language (*ridna mova*), up from 57% in July 2012, including 30% of Russian speakers.[[84]](#cite_note-:0-86)[[85]](#cite_note-:1-87)

Ukrainian has become popular in other countries through movies and songs performed in the Ukrainian language. The most popular [Ukrainian rock](/wiki/Ukrainian_rock "Ukrainian rock") bands, such as [Okean Elzy](/wiki/Okean_Elzy "Okean Elzy"), [Vopli Vidopliassova](/wiki/Vopli_Vidopliassova "Vopli Vidopliassova"), and [BoomBox](/wiki/BoomBox_(Ukrainian_band) "BoomBox (Ukrainian band)") perform regularly in tours across Europe, Israel, North America and especially Russia. In countries with significant Ukrainian populations, bands singing in the Ukrainian language sometimes reach top places on the charts, such as [Enej](/w/index.php?title=Enej&action=edit&redlink=1 "Enej (page does not exist)") (a band from Poland). Other notable Ukrainian-language bands are [The Ukrainians](/wiki/The_Ukrainians "The Ukrainians") from the United Kingdom, [Klooch](/w/index.php?title=Klooch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Klooch (page does not exist)") from Canada, [Ukrainian Village Band](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Village_Band&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ukrainian Village Band (page does not exist)") from the United States, and the [Kuban Cossack Choir](/wiki/Kuban_Cossack_Choir "Kuban Cossack Choir") from the [Kuban](/wiki/Kuban "Kuban") region in Russia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Argots

Oleksa Horbach's 1951 study of argots analyzed historical primary sources (argots of professionals, thugs, prisoners, homeless, school children, etc.) paying special attention to etymological features of argots, word formation and borrowing patterns depending on the source-language (Church Slavonic, Russian, Czech, Polish, Romani, Greek, Romanian, Hungarian, German).[[88]](#cite_note-90)

### Dialects

Main article: [Ukrainian dialects](/wiki/Ukrainian_dialects "Ukrainian dialects")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Map_of_Ukrainian_dialects_en.png/250px-Map_of_Ukrainian_dialects_en.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ukrainian_dialects_en.png)

Several modern [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") of Ukrainian exist.[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92)

* *Northern (Polissian)* dialects:[[91]](#cite_note-93)
  + *(3) Eastern Polissian* is spoken in [Chernihiv](/wiki/Chernihiv_oblast "Chernihiv oblast") (excluding the southeastern districts), in the northern part of [Sumy](/wiki/Sumy_Oblast "Sumy Oblast"), and in the southeastern portion of the [Kyiv Oblast](/wiki/Kyiv_Oblast "Kyiv Oblast") as well as in the adjacent areas of Russia, which include the southwestern part of the [Bryansk Oblast](/wiki/Bryansk_Oblast "Bryansk Oblast") (the area around [Starodub](/wiki/Starodub "Starodub")), as well as in some places in the [Kursk](/wiki/Kursk_Oblast "Kursk Oblast"), [Voronezh](/wiki/Voronezh_Oblast "Voronezh Oblast") and [Belgorod](/wiki/Belgorod_Oblast "Belgorod Oblast") Oblasts.[[92]](#cite_note-94) No linguistic border can be defined. The vocabulary approaches Russian as the language approaches the Russian Federation. Both Ukrainian and Russian grammar sets can be applied to this dialect.[[93]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-95)
  + *(2) Central Polissian* is spoken in the northwestern part of the [Kyiv Oblast](/wiki/Kyiv_Oblast "Kyiv Oblast"), in the northern part of [Zhytomyr](/wiki/Zhytomyr_Oblast "Zhytomyr Oblast") and the northeastern part of the [Rivne Oblast](/wiki/Rivne_Oblast "Rivne Oblast").[[94]](#cite_note-96)
  + *(1) West Polissian* is spoken in the northern part of the [Volyn Oblast](/wiki/Volyn_Oblast "Volyn Oblast"), the northwestern part of the [Rivne Oblast](/wiki/Rivne_Oblast "Rivne Oblast"), and in the adjacent districts of the [Brest Region](/wiki/Brest_Region "Brest Region") in Belarus. The dialect spoken in Belarus uses Belarusian grammar and thus is considered by some to be a dialect of Belarusian.[[95]](#cite_note-97)
* *Southeastern* dialects:[[96]](#cite_note-98)
  + *(4) Middle Dnieprian* is the basis of the [Standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") Literary Ukrainian. It is spoken in the central part of Ukraine, primarily in the southern and eastern part of the [Kyiv Oblast](/wiki/Kyiv_Oblast "Kyiv Oblast"). In addition, the dialects spoken in [Cherkasy](/wiki/Cherkasy_Oblast "Cherkasy Oblast"), [Poltava](/wiki/Poltava_Oblast "Poltava Oblast"), and Kyiv regions are considered to be close to "standard" Ukrainian.
  + *(5) Slobodan* is spoken in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv_Oblast "Kharkiv Oblast"), [Sumy](/wiki/Sumy_Oblast "Sumy Oblast"), [Luhansk](/wiki/Luhansk_Oblast "Luhansk Oblast"), and the northern part of [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk_Oblast "Donetsk Oblast"), as well as in the [Voronezh](/wiki/Voronezh_Oblast "Voronezh Oblast") and [Belgorod](/wiki/Belgorod_Oblast "Belgorod Oblast") regions of Russia.[[97]](#cite_note-99) This dialect is formed from a gradual mixture of Russian and Ukrainian, with progressively more Russian in the northern and eastern parts of the region. Thus, there is no linguistic border between Russian and Ukrainian, and, thus, both grammar sets can be applied.[[93]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-95)
  + A *(6) Steppe* dialect is spoken in southern and southeastern Ukraine. This dialect was originally the main language of the [Zaporozhian Cossacks](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Cossacks "Zaporozhian Cossacks").[[98]](#cite_note-100)
  + A *Kuban* dialect related to or based on the Steppe dialect is often referred to as *[Balachka](/wiki/Balachka "Balachka")* and is spoken by the [Kuban Cossacks](/wiki/Kuban_Cossacks "Kuban Cossacks") in the [Kuban](/wiki/Kuban "Kuban") region in Russia by the descendants of the [Zaporozhian Cossacks](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Cossacks "Zaporozhian Cossacks"), who settled in that area in the late 18th century. It was formed from a gradual mixture of Russian into Ukrainian. This dialect features the use of some Russian vocabulary along with some Russian grammar.[[99]](#cite_note-Zakharchenko-101) There are three main variants, which have been grouped together according to location.[[100]](#cite_note-102)
* *Southwestern* dialects:[[101]](#cite_note-103)
  + *(13) Boyko* is spoken by the [Boyko people](/wiki/Boyko "Boyko") on the northern side of the Carpathian Mountains in the [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast") and [Ivano-Frankivsk](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk_Oblast "Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast") Oblasts. It can also be heard across the border in the [Subcarpathian Voivodeship](/wiki/Subcarpathian_Voivodeship "Subcarpathian Voivodeship") of Poland.
  + *(12) Hutsul* is spoken by the [Hutsul people](/wiki/Hutsuls "Hutsuls") on the northern slopes of the Carpathian Mountains, in the extreme southern parts of the [Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk_Oblast "Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast"), and in parts of the [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") and [Transcarpathian](/wiki/Transcarpathian_Oblast "Transcarpathian Oblast") Oblasts.
  + *Lemko* is spoken by the [Lemko people](/wiki/Lemkos "Lemkos"), whose [homeland](/wiki/Lemkivshchyna "Lemkivshchyna") rests outside the borders of Ukraine in the [Prešov Region](/wiki/Pre%C5%A1ov_Region "Prešov Region") of [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") along the southern side of the Carpathian Mountains, and in the southeast of modern Poland, along the northern sides of the Carpathians.
  + *(8) Podillian* is spoken in the southern parts of the [Vinnytsia](/wiki/Vinnytsia_Oblast "Vinnytsia Oblast") and [Khmelnytskyi](/wiki/Khmelnytskyi_Oblast "Khmelnytskyi Oblast") Oblasts, in the northern part of the [Odesa Oblast](/wiki/Odesa_Oblast "Odesa Oblast"), and in the adjacent districts of the [Cherkasy Oblast](/wiki/Cherkasy_Oblast "Cherkasy Oblast"), the [Kirovohrad Oblast](/wiki/Kirovohrad_Oblast "Kirovohrad Oblast"), and the [Mykolaiv Oblast](/wiki/Mykolaiv_Oblast "Mykolaiv Oblast").[[102]](#cite_note-104)
  + *(7) Volynian* is spoken in [Rivne](/wiki/Rivne_Oblast "Rivne Oblast") and [Volyn](/wiki/Volyn_Oblast "Volyn Oblast"), as well as in parts of [Zhytomyr](/wiki/Zhytomyr_Oblast "Zhytomyr Oblast") and [Ternopil](/wiki/Ternopil_Oblast "Ternopil Oblast"). It is also used in [Chełm](/wiki/Che%C5%82m "Chełm"), Poland.
  + *(11) Pokuttia (Bukovinian)* is spoken in the [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") of Ukraine. This dialect has some distinct vocabulary borrowed from [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language").
  + *(9) Upper Dniestrian* (Kresy) is considered to be the main Galician dialect, spoken in the [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast"), [Ternopil](/wiki/Ternopil_Oblast "Ternopil Oblast"), and [Ivano-Frankivsk](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk_Oblast "Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast") Oblasts. Its distinguishing characteristics are the influence of Polish and the German vocabulary, which is reminiscent of the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian "Austro-Hungarian") rule. Some of the distinct words used in this dialect can be found here.[[103]](#cite_note-105)
  + *(10) Upper Sannian* is spoken in the border area between Ukraine and Poland in the San river valley.
* The *[Rusyn language](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language")* is considered by Ukrainian linguists to be a dialect of Ukrainian as well:[[104]](#cite_note-106)
  + *Dolinian Rusyn or Subcarpathian Rusyn* is spoken in the [Transcarpathian Oblast](/wiki/Transcarpathian_Oblast "Transcarpathian Oblast").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + *[Pannonian or Bačka Rusyn](/wiki/Pannonian_Rusyn_language "Pannonian Rusyn language")* is spoken in northwestern [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") and eastern [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"). [Rusin language](/wiki/Rusin_language "Rusin language") of the Bačka dialect is one of the official languages of the Serbian Autonomous Province of [Vojvodina](/wiki/Vojvodina "Vojvodina").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + *Pryashiv Rusyn* is the Rusyn spoken in the Prešov (in Ukrainian: [Pryashiv](/wiki/Pryashiv "Pryashiv")) region of Slovakia, as well as by some émigré communities, primarily in the United States of America.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

All the countries neighbouring Ukraine (except for Hungary) historically have regions with a sizable Ukrainian population and therefore Ukrainian language speakers. Ukrainian is an official minority language in Belarus, Romania, and Moldova.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ukrainian diaspora

Ukrainian is also spoken by a large [émigrée](/wiki/%C3%89migr%C3%A9 "Émigré") population, particularly [in Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Ukrainian "Canadian Ukrainian"), the United States, and several countries of South America like Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The founders of this population primarily emigrated from [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)"), which used to be part of [Austro-Hungary](/wiki/Austro-Hungary "Austro-Hungary") before World War I, and belonged to Poland between the World Wars. The language spoken by most of them is the Galician dialect of Ukrainian from the first half of the 20th century. Compared with modern Ukrainian, the vocabulary of Ukrainians outside Ukraine reflects less influence of Russian, but often contains many [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords") from the local language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Most of the countries where it is spoken are [ex-USSR](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"), where many Ukrainians have migrated. Canada and the United States are also home to a large Ukrainian population. Broken up by country (to the nearest thousand):[[105]](#cite_note-e27-107)

1. Russia 1,129,838 (according to the [2010 census](/wiki/Russian_Census_(2010) "Russian Census (2010)"));[[106]](#cite_note-rus-108)
2. Canada 200,525[[107]](#cite_note-109) (67,665 spoken at home[[108]](#cite_note-110) in 2001, 148,000 spoken as "mother tongue" in 2001)[[109]](#cite_note-111)

Ukrainian is declaratively proclaimed as one of three official languages of the breakaway Moldovan region of [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria "Transnistria").[[110]](#cite_note-112)

Ukrainian is widely spoken within the 400,000-strong (in 1994) [Ukrainian community in Brazil](/wiki/Ukrainian_Brazilian "Ukrainian Brazilian").[[111]](#cite_note-Boruszenko-113) It is the official language in [Prudentópolis](/wiki/Prudent%C3%B3polis "Prudentópolis") alongside [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language").[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)

## Language structure

:   *Cyrillic letters in this article are [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian") using [scientific transliteration](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian#Scientific_transliteration "Romanization of Ukrainian").*

### Grammar

Further information: [Ukrainian grammar](/wiki/Ukrainian_grammar "Ukrainian grammar")

Ukrainian is a [fusional](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language"), [nominative–accusative](/wiki/Nominative%E2%80%93accusative_alignment "Nominative–accusative alignment"), [satellite-framed](/wiki/Verb_framing "Verb framing") language. It exhibits [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction"), and is [null-subject](/wiki/Null-subject_language "Null-subject language"). The canonical word order of Ukrainian is [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object").[[115]](#cite_note-117) Other [word orders](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") are common due to the free word order enabled by Ukrainian's [inflectional](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Nouns have one of 3 [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"): masculine, feminine, neuter; nouns [decline](/wiki/Declension "Declension") for:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* 7 [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_cases "Grammatical cases"): [nominative](/wiki/Nominative "Nominative"), [accusative](/wiki/Accusative "Accusative"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive"), [dative](/wiki/Dative "Dative"), [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case"), [locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case"), [vocative](/wiki/Vocative "Vocative");
* 2 [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"): [singular](/wiki/Grammatical_number#singular_vs_plural "Grammatical number"), [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural").

[Adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") [agree](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") with nouns in gender, [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"), and [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Verbs [conjugate](/wiki/Grammatical_conjugation "Grammatical conjugation") for:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* 4 [tenses](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense"): [past](/wiki/Past_tense "Past tense"), [pluperfect](/wiki/Pluperfect "Pluperfect"), [present](/wiki/Present_tense "Present tense"), [future](/wiki/Future_tense "Future tense");
* 2 [voices](/wiki/Grammatical_voice "Grammatical voice"): [active](/wiki/Active_voice "Active voice"), [mediopassive](/wiki/Mediopassive_voice "Mediopassive voice");
* 3 [persons](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"): first, second, third;
* 2 [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"): [singular](/wiki/Grammatical_number#singular_vs_plural "Grammatical number"), [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural").

Ukrainian verbs come in [aspect pairs](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect_in_Slavic_languages "Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages"): [perfective](/wiki/Perfective_aspect "Perfective aspect"), and [imperfective](/wiki/Imperfective_aspect "Imperfective aspect"). Pairs are usually formed by a [prepositional](/wiki/Prepositional "Prepositional") prefix and occasionally a [root change](/wiki/Apophony "Apophony"). The [past tense](/wiki/Past_tense "Past tense") agrees with its [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") in number and gender (but *not* [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person")), having developed from the [perfect](/wiki/Perfect_(grammar) "Perfect (grammar)") [participle](/wiki/Participle "Participle").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Old East Slavic and Russian *o* in syllables ending in a consonant, often correspond to a Ukrainian *i*, as in *pod* → *pid* (під, 'under'). Thus, in the declension of nouns, the *o* can re-appear when it is no longer located in a closed syllable, such as *rik* (рік, 'year') ([nom](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case")): *rotsi* ([loc](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case")) (році). Similarly, some words can have *і* in some cases when most of the cases have *o*, for example *слово* (nominative singular), *слова* (nominative plural) but *слiв* (genitive plural).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ukrainian case endings are somewhat different from Old East Slavic, and the vocabulary includes a large overlay of Polish terminology. Russian *na pervom etaže* 'on the first floor' is in the locative (prepositional) case. The Ukrainian corresponding expression is *na peršomu poversi* (на першому поверсі). *-omu* is the standard locative (prepositional) ending, but variants in *-im* are common in dialect and poetry, and allowed by the standards bodies. The *kh* of Ukrainian *poverkh* (поверх) has mutated into *s* under the influence of the soft vowel *i* (*k* is similarly mutable into *c* in final positions).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
