The poem "Gleams of Thunderstorm" by [Emma Andijewska](/wiki/Emma_Andijewska "Emma Andijewska") read in Ukrainian

The Ukrainian language has six vowels, /i/, /u/, /ɪ/, /ɛ/, /ɔ/, /a/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A number of the consonants come in three forms: hard, soft ([palatalized](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)")) and [long](/wiki/Geminate_consonant "Geminate consonant"), for example, /l/, /lʲ/, and /lː/ or /n/, /nʲ/, and /nː/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The letter ⟨г⟩ represents the [voiced glottal fricative](/wiki/Voiced_glottal_fricative "Voiced glottal fricative") /ɦ/, often transliterated as Latin *h*. It is the [voiced](/wiki/Voiced_consonant "Voiced consonant") equivalent of English /h/. Russian speakers from Ukraine often use the soft Ukrainian /ɦ/ in place of Russian /ɡ/, which comes from northern dialects of Old East Slavic. The Ukrainian alphabet has the additional letter ⟨[ґ](/wiki/%D2%90 "Ґ")⟩ for /ɡ/, which appears in a few native words such as ґринджоли *gryndžoly* 'sleigh' and ґудзик *gudzyk* 'button'. However, /ɡ/ appears almost exclusively in [loan words](/wiki/Loan_words "Loan words"), and is usually simply written ⟨г⟩. For example, loanwords from English on public signs usually use ⟨г⟩ for both English *g* and *h*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another phonetic divergence between the Ukrainian and Russian languages is the pronunciation of Cyrillic ⟨[в](/wiki/%D0%92 "В")⟩ *v/w*. While in standard Russian it represents /v/, in many Ukrainian dialects it denotes /w/ (following a vowel and preceding a consonant (cluster), either within a word or at a word boundary, it denotes the allophone [u̯], and like the off-glide in the English words "flow" and "cow", it forms a [diphthong](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") with the preceding vowel). Native Russian speakers will pronounce the Ukrainian ⟨в⟩ as [v], which is one way to tell the two groups apart. As with ⟨г⟩ above, Ukrainians use ⟨в⟩ to render both English *v* and *w*; Russians occasionally use ⟨у⟩ for *w* instead.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Unlike Russian and most other modern Slavic languages, Ukrainian does not have [final devoicing](/wiki/Final_devoicing "Final devoicing").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Alphabet

Main article: [Ukrainian alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_alphabet "Ukrainian alphabet")

The Ukrainian alphabet

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| А а | Б б | В в | Г г | Ґ ґ | Д д | Е е | Є є | Ж ж | З з | И и |
| І і | Ї ї | Й й | К к | Л л | М м | Н н | О о | П п | Р р | С с |
| Т т | У у | Ф ф | Х х | Ц ц | Ч ч | Ш ш | Щ щ | Ь ь | Ю ю | Я я |

Ukrainian is written in a version of [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script"), consisting of 33 letters, representing 38 [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"); an [apostrophe](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe") is also used. Ukrainian orthography is based on the phonemic principle, with one letter generally corresponding to one phoneme, although there are a number of exceptions. The orthography also has cases where the semantic, historical, and morphological principles are applied.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The modern Ukrainian alphabet is the result of a number of proposed alphabetic reforms from the 19th and early 20th centuries, in Ukraine under the Russian Empire, in Austrian Galicia, and later in Soviet Ukraine. A unified Ukrainian alphabet (the *[Skrypnykivka](/wiki/Ukrainian_orthography_of_1928 "Ukrainian orthography of 1928")*, after [Mykola Skrypnyk](/wiki/Mykola_Skrypnyk "Mykola Skrypnyk")) was officially established at a 1927 international Orthographic Conference in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"), during the period of [Ukrainization](/wiki/Ukrainization "Ukrainization") in Soviet Ukraine. But the policy was reversed in the 1930s, and the Soviet Ukrainian orthography diverged from that used by the [diaspora](/wiki/Ukrainian_diaspora "Ukrainian diaspora"). The Ukrainian letter [ge](/wiki/Ghe_with_upturn "Ghe with upturn") *ґ* was banned in the Soviet Union from 1933 until the period of Glasnost in 1990.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

The letter щ represents two consonants [ʃt͡ʃ]. The combination of [j] with some of the vowels is also represented by a single letter ([ja] = я, [je] = є, [ji] or [jı̽] = ї, [ju] = ю), while [jɔ] = йо and the rare regional [jɨ] = йи are written using two letters. These [iotated](/wiki/Iotated "Iotated") vowel letters and a special [soft sign](/wiki/Soft_sign "Soft sign") change a preceding consonant from hard to soft. An [apostrophe](/wiki/Ukrainian_apostrophe "Ukrainian apostrophe") is used to indicate the hardness of the sound in the cases when normally the vowel would change the consonant to soft; in other words, it functions like the [yer](/wiki/Yer "Yer") in the Russian alphabet.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A consonant letter is doubled to indicate that the sound is doubled, or long.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The phonemes [d͡z] and [d͡ʒ] do not have dedicated letters in the alphabet and are rendered with the [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") дз and дж, respectively. [d͡z] is equivalent to English *ds* in *pods*, [d͡ʒ] is equivalent to *j* in *jump*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As in Russian, the [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") may be used to denote vowel stress.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Orthography

Main article: [Ukrainian orthography](/wiki/Ukrainian_orthography "Ukrainian orthography")

See also: [Ukrainian orthography of 2019](/wiki/Ukrainian_orthography_of_2019 "Ukrainian orthography of 2019")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/KB_Ukrainian.svg/250px-KB_Ukrainian.svg.png)](/wiki/File:KB_Ukrainian.svg)

A Ukrainian keyboard layout

Spelling search,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] which began in the late 18th century with the emergence of modern literary language, led to the emergence of several spelling options. In particular, there was the spelling system of Oleksii Pavlovskyi, the [spelling version of "Mermaid of the Dniester"](/wiki/Shashkevychivka "Shashkevychivka") (1837), Kulishivka (P. Kulish's spelling system), [Drahomanivka](/wiki/Drahomanivka "Drahomanivka") (produced in Kyiv in the 1870s by a group of cultural figures led by linguist P. Zhytetskyi, which included and M. Drahomanov), [Zhelekhivka](/wiki/Zhelekhivka "Zhelekhivka") (system of Yevhen Zhelekhovskyi (1886), enshrined in the *Ruthenian Grammar* by Smal-Stotskyi and [Theodore Gartner](/wiki/Theodor_Gartner "Theodor Gartner") 1893).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Borys Hrinchenko](/wiki/Borys_Grinchenko "Borys Grinchenko") used some corrections in the fundamental four-volume *Dictionary of the Ukrainian Language* (1907–1909). Most of the spelling rules (practically based on phonetics – "write as you hear") used in Hrinchenko's dictionary are still valid. Hrinchenko's work became an informal spelling and model for Ukrainian writers and publications from 1907 until the creation of the first official Ukrainian spelling in 1918.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 17 January 1918, the [Central Rada of Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Council_of_Ukraine "Central Council of Ukraine") issued the "Main Rules of Ukrainian orthography", which, however, did not cover the entire scope of the language. On 17 May 1919, the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences approved the "Main Rules of Ukrainian Orthography", which became the basis for all subsequent revisions and amendments.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 23 July 1925, the Council of People's Commissars of the USSR decided to organize a State Commission for the Organization of Ukrainian Spelling (State Spelling Commission). It included more than 20 academics from the USSR, who also expressed a desire to invite representatives of Western Ukraine: Smal-Stotskyi, [Volodymyr Hnatiuk](/wiki/Volodymyr_Hnatiuk "Volodymyr Hnatiuk") and Vasyl Simovych.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After almost a year of work in April 1926, the "Project of Ukrainian Spelling" was published to acquaint the general public with the new system. After several months of discussion and consideration of the project at the All-Ukrainian Spelling Conference (26 May – 6 June 1927), the Ukrainian orthography of 1928 was adopted in accordance with the RNC resolution of 6 September 1928. It went down in history as "Kharkiv" or "Skrypnik orthography" – from the place of creation, or from the surname of Skrypnyk.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1929, Hryhorii Holoskevych published the *Ukrainian Spelling Dictionary* (about 40,000 words), agreed with the full spelling produced by the State Spelling Commission and approved by the People's Commissar for Education (6 September 1928).[[117]](#cite_note-119)

In 1933, a spelling commission headed by Andrii Khvylia branded the Ukrainian orthography of 1928 as "nationalist", immediately stopped publishing any dictionaries, and without any discussion, in a very short time (five months), created a [new spelling](/wiki/Ukrainian_orthography_of_1933 "Ukrainian orthography of 1933") that unified as never before the Ukrainian and Russian languages. The letter ґ was removed from the alphabet, and Ukrainian scientific terminology was revised and harmonized with Russian-Ukrainian dictionaries (the Institute of Ukrainian Scientific Language was abolished in 1930). This version of the spelling was approved by the resolution of the People's Commissar of Education of the USSR of 5 September 1933.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some minor changes were made in the spelling of 1946 and 1959 (published the following year). It was connected with the document "The rules of Russian spelling and punctuation", published in 1956. From 1960 until 1990, the 1960 edition was the official standard.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the beginning of "perestroika", the issue of improving Ukrainian spelling became relevant again: the editing of the spelling code was started by the Orthographic Commission at the LMM of the USSR Academy of Sciences. The project was also discussed in the newly established Ukrainian Language Society. T. Shevchenko (headed by [Dmytro Pavlychko](/wiki/Dmytro_Pavlychko "Dmytro Pavlychko")). The new version was approved on 14 November 1989, and published in 1990. The main achievements were the restoration of the letter ґ and the [accusative case](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case") (in Soviet times it was optional and was called the *accusative form*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Today, despite the existence of the official spelling of the Ukrainian language, it is not the only spelling standard in use. Even in Ukraine itself, many publishers and publications use other versions of the spelling, which either tend to "skrypnykivka", or else differ from the official rules of transmission of words of foreign origin.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 22 May 2019, the [Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") approved a [new version of the orthography](/wiki/Ukrainian_orthography_of_2019 "Ukrainian orthography of 2019") prepared by the Ukrainian National Commission on Spelling. The new edition brought to life some features of orthography in 1928, which were part of the Ukrainian orthographic tradition. At the same time, the commission was guided by the understanding that the language practice of Ukrainians in the second half of the 20th to the beginning of the 21st century has already become part of the Ukrainian orthographic tradition.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

### Vocabulary

*The Dictionary of the Ukrainian Language*, in 11 volumes, contains 253,000 entries.[[119]](#cite_note-121) Lexical card catalog of the Ukrainian Institute of Language Studies has six million cards.[[120]](#cite_note-122) As mentioned at the top of the article, Ukrainian is most closely related lexically to Belarusian, and is also closer to Polish than to Russian (for example, можливість, *mozhlyvistʹ*, "possibility", and Polish *możliwość*, but Russian возможность, *vozmozhnostʹ*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The standard Ukrainian language which is based on the Kyiv–Poltava dialect has a plethora of [false friends](/wiki/False_friend "False friend") with the standard Russian language which is based on the dialect of Moscow. Many people intentionally do or do not use them, causing their language shift into what is known as [Surzhyk](/wiki/Surzhyk "Surzhyk"), where the meaning of some words mimicking Russian could be understood out of context rather than their literal meaning in Ukrainian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

False friend samples

| English | Ukrainian | Surzhyk | Russian |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| to cancel | скасовувати *skasovuvaty* | відміняти *vidminiaty* | отменять *otmenyatʹ* |
| to conjugate | відміняти *vidminiaty* | спрягати *spriahaty* | спрягать *spryagatʹ* |
| gentle | лагідний *lahidnyi* | ласкавий *laskavyi* | ласковый *laskovyy* |
| kind | ласкавий *laskavyi* | добродушний *dobrodushnyi* | добродушный *dobrodushnyy* |

## Classification

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Geographical_distribution_of_extant_Slavic_and_East_Baltic_languages_2015.png/250px-Geographical_distribution_of_extant_Slavic_and_East_Baltic_languages_2015.png)](/wiki/File:Geographical_distribution_of_extant_Slavic_and_East_Baltic_languages_2015.png)

Ethnographic Map of Slavic and Baltic Languages

Ukrainian has varying degrees of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with other [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"). It is closely related to other [East Slavic languages](/wiki/East_Slavic_languages "East Slavic languages") with high levels of mutual intelligibility.[[7]](#cite_note-classification145-9) Ukrainian is considered to be most closely related to Belarusian.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The separation of the East Slavic languages is considered to be relatively recent.[[7]](#cite_note-classification145-9) In the 19th century, the question of whether the Ukrainian, [Belarusian](/wiki/Belorusian_language "Belorusian language") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") languages were dialects of a single language or three separate languages was actively discussed, with the debate affected by linguistic and political factors.[[7]](#cite_note-classification145-9) The political situation (Ukraine and Belarus being mainly part of the Russian Empire at the time) and the historical existence of the medieval state of Kievan Rusʹ, which occupied large parts of these three nations, led to the creation of the common classification known later as the East Slavic languages. The underlying theory of the grouping is their descent from a common ancestor. In modern times, Ukrainian, Russian, and Belarusian are usually listed by linguists as separate languages.[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[105]](#cite_note-e27-107)

The Ukrainians were predominantly [peasants](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") and [petits bourgeois](/wiki/Petit_bourgeois "Petit bourgeois"). In 1897, 93% of Ukrainians were peasants.[[14]](#cite_note-WUkraineL-16) As a result, the Ukrainian language was mostly vernacular and few earlier literary works from the period can be found. In the cities, Ukrainian coexisted with [Church Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic_language "Church Slavonic language")—a literary language of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion") that evolved from Old Church Slavonic—and later Polish and Russian, both languages which were more often used in formal writing and communication during that time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Differences from other Slavic languages

The Ukrainian language has the following similarities with and differences from other Slavic languages:

* Like all Slavic languages with the exception of [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), Belarusian, standard written Slovak[[c]](#cite_note-126) and [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), the Ukrainian language has preserved the Common Slavic [vocative case](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case"). When addressing one's sister (*sestra*) she is referred to as *sestro.* In the Russian language the vocative case has been almost entirely replaced by the nominative (except for a handful of vestigial forms, e.g. *Bozhe* "God!" and *Gospodi* "Lord!").[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with all Slavic languages other than Russian, Slovak and Slovene, has retained the Common [Slavic second palatalization](/wiki/Slavic_second_palatalization "Slavic second palatalization") of the velars \*k, \*g and \*x in front of the secondary vowel \*ě of the dative and locative ending in the female [declension](/wiki/Declension "Declension"), resulting in the final sequences -cě, -zě, and -sě. For example, *ruka* (hand) becomes *ruci* in Ukrainian. In Russian, the dative and locative of *ruka* is *ruke.*
* The Ukrainian language, in common with Serbo-Croatian and Slovene, has developed the ending -*mo* for first-person plurals in verbs (*khodymo* for "we walk").[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127) In all cases, it resulted from lengthening of the Common Slavic -*mŭ*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Ukrainian_language#Dubious "Talk:Ukrainian language")*]
* The Ukrainian language, along with Russian and Belarusian, has changed the Common Slavic word-initial *ye*- into *o*, such as in the words *ozero* (lake) and *odyn* (one).[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with Czech, Slovak, Upper Sorbian, Belarusian and southern Russian dialects, has changed the Common Slavic "g" into an "h" sound (for example, *noha* – leg).[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with some northern Russian and Croatian dialects, has transformed the Common Slavic *yě* into *i* (for example, *lis* – forest).[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with Russian, Belarusian, Bulgarian, Serbo-Croatian, Macedonian, and Slovene, has simplified the Common Slavic *tl* and *dl* into *l* (for example, *mela* – she swept").[[124]](#cite_note-comparison-127)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with the most of Slavic ones, is a [stress-timed language](/wiki/Isochrony "Isochrony"), in which syllables may last different amounts of time, but there is perceived to be a fairly constant amount of time (on average) between consecutive stressed syllables.[[125]](#cite_note-128)
* The Ukrainian language, in common with all modern Slavic languages other than Bulgarian and Macedonian, does not use [articles](/wiki/Article_(linguistics) "Article (linguistics)").
* Other Slavic *o* in closed syllables, i.e., syllables ending in a consonant, in many cases corresponds to a Ukrainian *i*, as in *pod* → *pid* (під, 'under'). This also includes place names such as Lviv (Львів in Ukrainian), Lwów in Polish, and Львов (Lvov) in Russian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Unlike all other Slavic languages, Ukrainian has a synthetic future (also termed inflectional future) tense which developed through the erosion and [cliticization](/wiki/Clitic "Clitic") of the verb "to have" (or possibly "to take"): *pysat-ymu* (infinitive-future-1st sg.) *I will write*.[[126]](#cite_note-Languages_of_Europe-129) Although the inflectional future (based on the verb 'to have') is characteristic of [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), Ukrainian linguist A. Danylenko argues that Ukrainian differs from Romance in the choice of auxiliary, which should be interpreted as 'to take' and not 'to have.' He states that Late Common Slavic (LCS) had three verbs with the same Proto-Indo-European root \**h₁em-*:

* a determined imperfective LCS \*jęti: \*jĭmǫ 'to take' (later superseded by numerous prefixed perfectives)
* an indetermined imperfective LCS \*jĭmati: jemljǫ 'to take' (which would not take any prefixes)
* an imperfective LCS \*jĭměti: \*jĭmamĭ 'to hold, own, have'

The three verbs became conflated in East Slavic due to morphological overlap, in particular of \*iměti "to have" and \*jati "to take" as exemplified in the Middle Ukrainian homonymic imut’ from both iměti (< \*jĭměti) and jati (< \*jęti). Analogous grammaticalization of the type take ("to take", "to seize") > future is found in [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") and [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language").[[127]](#cite_note-Danylenko-130)

## Sample text

Ukrainian pronunciation

Article 1 of the *[Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights")* in Ukrainian:

> ***Всі люди народжуються вільними і рівними у своїй гідності та правах. Вони наділені розумом і совістю і повинні діяти у відношенні один до одного в дусі братерства.***[[128]](#cite_note-131)

The [romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian") of the text into [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"):

> *Vsi lyudy narodzhuyutʹsya vilʹnymy i rivnymy u svoyiy hidnosti ta pravakh. Vony nadileni rozumom i sovistyu i povynni diyaty u vidnoshenni odyn do odnoho v dusi braterstva.*

Article 1 of the *Universal Declaration of Human Rights* in English:[[129]](#cite_note-132)

> All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.
